title: Momentum and Impulse
learningObjectives: Use conservation of momentum to solve collision problems
Distinguish between elastic and inelastic collisions
introText: Momentum is the 'quantity of motion' — it combines both mass and velocity. Conservation of momentum is one of the most powerful principles in physics: the total momentum of a closed system never changes.

steps.heading: Linear Momentum
steps.body:
**Momentum (p) = mv**

• p = momentum (kg·m/s)
• m = mass (kg)
• v = velocity (m/s)

Momentum is a vector — direction matters. In 1D, a negative momentum means the object moves in the negative direction.

The SI unit kg·m/s is equivalent to N·s (Newton-second).
steps.deepDive: Momentum appears in Newton's Second Law in its most general form: F = dp/dt (force equals rate of change of momentum). For constant mass, F = m(dv/dt) = ma, which is the familiar form.

steps.heading: Impulse
steps.body:
**Impulse (J) = FΔt = Δp = mv − mu**

Impulse is the change in momentum caused by a force over a time interval. It equals the area under a force-time graph.

**Impulse-momentum theorem:** J = Δp

Example: A cricket ball (mass 0.16 kg) is hit from rest to 30 m/s.
Impulse = Δp = 0.16 × 30 − 0 = 4.8 N·s

If contact time = 0.002 s:
Force = J/Δt = 4.8/0.002 = 2400 N
steps.deepDive: Crumple zones in cars increase the collision time Δt, reducing the average force for the same impulse/change in momentum. Air bags do the same for the occupants. This is why Δt matters so much in safety engineering.

steps.heading: Conservation of Momentum
steps.body:
**In a closed system (no external net forces), total momentum is conserved:**

p_before = p_after
m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂

**Elastic collision:** KE is also conserved.
**Inelastic collision:** KE is not conserved (converted to heat, sound, etc.).
**Perfectly inelastic:** Objects stick together after collision, maximum KE loss.

Example: A 3 kg cart at 4 m/s collides with a stationary 1 kg cart and they stick together.
Before: p = 3 × 4 + 0 = 12 kg·m/s
After: (3 + 1)v = 12 → v = 3 m/s

steps.questionText: A 0.5 kg ball moving at 6 m/s east hits a stationary 1.5 kg ball. They stick together. What is their combined velocity after collision?
A 70 kg person jumps backward off a 200 kg stationary boat at 2 m/s. At what speed does the boat move forward?
steps.explanation: Initial p = 0. So 70(−2) + 200v = 0 → v = 140/200 = 0.7 m/s forward.

questions.questionText: A 1000 kg car travelling at 20 m/s is brought to rest in 4 s by brakes. What average braking force acts?
In a perfectly elastic head-on collision between two equal masses where one is initially stationary, what happens?
options: Both move at half the initial speed
The moving one stops and the stationary one moves at the original speed
The moving one bounces back at the same speed
questions.explanation: For equal masses in a perfectly elastic collision, they swap velocities. The moving mass stops and the stationary one moves at the original speed.
